prompt: How many sides does a triangle have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Absolute floor anchor — every model should get this right.

prompt: What is the square root of 100? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple perfect square root — calibration floor.

prompt: How many days are in a standard (non-leap) year? Answer with digits only.
note: Universal knowledge anchor.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 3? Answer with digits only.
note: Low-order exponent — easy baseline.

prompt: How many continents are there on Earth? Answer with a digit only.
note: Universal geographical knowledge — calibration floor anchor.

prompt: What is the chemical formula for water? Answer with the formula only.
note: Universal science anchor — models always know this.

prompt: How many hours are in one day? Answer with digits only.
note: Absolute floor — calibration baseline.

prompt: What is the capital of France? Answer with the city name only.
note: Quintessential easy anchor — every model knows this.

prompt: How many players are on a standard basketball team on the court at one time? Answer with a digit only.
note: Easy floor anchor — 5 players per team on court.

prompt: What is 12 times 12? Answer with digits only.
note: Easy multiplication anchor — 12² = 144.

prompt: How many bones are in the human hand (not including the wrist)? Answer with a number only.
note: The hand has 27 bones total including wrist (8 carpals). Excluding wrist: 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges = 19. Models often confuse with 27 (whole hand+wrist). Precision required.

prompt: In what year did the Titanic sink? Answer with the year only.
note: Well-known historical event — solid medium anchor.

prompt: What is the largest planet in our solar system? Answer with the planet name only.
note: Standard astronomy fact — medium anchor.

prompt: Who wrote the novel '1984'? Answer with the author's name only.
note: Standard literary fact — medium calibration anchor.

prompt: What is the smallest country in the world by area? Answer with the country name only.
note: Standard geographic fact — Vatican City (Holy See) is 0.44 km².

prompt: What element has the atomic number 1? Answer with the element name only.
note: Standard chemistry — atomic number 1 is hydrogen.

prompt: What is the currency of Japan? Answer with the currency name only.
note: Standard economics fact — medium anchor.

prompt: How many Harry Potter main novels did J.K. Rowling write? Answer with a digit only.
note: Standard literary fact — 7 novels confirmed. Verified via Wikipedia and Bloomsbury.

prompt: In which country were the 2008 Summer Olympics held? Answer with the country name only.
note: Well-known sporting event — held in Beijing.

prompt: What is the speed of light in a vacuum, rounded to the nearest hundred thousand km/s? Answer with digits only.
note: ~299,792 km/s rounds to 300,000 km/s. Standard physics fact.

prompt: What gas do plants absorb from the atmosphere during photosynthesis? Answer with the gas name only.
note: Standard biology — plants absorb CO2 during photosynthesis.

prompt: How many players are on a standard soccer (association football) team on the field at one time? Answer with a digit only.
note: Standard sports fact — 11 per side.

prompt: What language has the most native speakers worldwide? Answer with one word only.
note: Mandarin Chinese has ~1.3 billion native speakers, more than any other single language. Verified via Ethnologue/Babbel.

prompt: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth? Answer with one word only.
note: Diamond scores 10 on Mohs scale — hardest natural substance.

prompt: How many sides does a standard stop sign have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Stop signs are octagons — 8 sides. Verified.

prompt: What is the tallest mountain in the world measured by height above sea level? Answer with the mountain name only.
note: Everest is highest above sea level at 8,848m. Note: Mauna Kea is taller from base, Chimborazo farthest from Earth's center — this is specifically above sea level.

prompt: How many bones are in the human hand including the wrist bones? Answer with a number only.
note: 8 carpals (wrist) + 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges = 27. Verified via multiple anatomy sources.

prompt: Which philosopher wrote 'Critique of Pure Reason'? Answer with the philosopher's name only.
note: Standard philosophy fact — Kant published Critique of Pure Reason in 1781.

prompt: What is the chemical symbol for sodium? Answer with the symbol only.
note: Standard chemistry — sodium symbol is Na (from Latin natrium).

prompt: What is the sum of the first 10 natural numbers (1 through 10)? Answer with digits only.
note: Sum formula: n(n+1)/2 = 10×11/2 = 55. Standard mathematical fact — medium difficulty.

prompt: How many keys does a standard piano have? Answer with digits only.
note: Standard piano has 88 keys (52 white, 36 black). Well-known music fact.

prompt: What is the largest internal organ in the human body? Answer with one word only.
note: The liver is the largest internal organ. (Skin is the largest organ overall but not internal.) Standard anatomy.

prompt: In what year did the Soviet Union officially dissolve? Answer with the year only.
note: The USSR officially dissolved on December 25, 1991. Standard historical fact.

prompt: What element does the chemical symbol 'Fe' represent? Answer with the element name only.
note: Fe = iron (from Latin ferrum). Standard chemistry.

prompt: What is the atomic number of carbon? Answer with a digit only.
note: Carbon is element 6 on the periodic table. Standard chemistry.

prompt: What is the name for a triangle where all three sides are equal? Answer with one word only.
note: Standard geometry vocabulary — equilateral triangle.

prompt: What is the tallest mountain measured by distance from Earth's center (not sea level)? Answer with the mountain name only.
reasoning_chain: Earth bulges at the equator. Chimborazo in Ecuador (6,268m above sea level) is closer to the equator than Everest. Due to Earth's equatorial bulge, Chimborazo's peak is 2,072m farther from Earth's center than Everest. NOAA and Mauna Kea Wikipedia confirm this.
note: Triple trap: Everest is tallest above sea level, Mauna Kea is tallest from ocean base, Chimborazo is farthest from Earth's center. This question specifically asks about Earth's center. Verified via NOAA.

prompt: How many days does it take the Moon to orbit Earth, approximately? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: The Moon's sidereal orbital period is 27.3 days. The synodic period (new moon to new moon) is 29.5 days. The question asks 'orbit Earth' which is the sidereal period = ~27 days.
note: Precision trap: 27 (sidereal, true orbital period) vs 29 (synodic, lunar month). Most people say 28-29 days thinking of the lunar cycle, not the orbital period.

prompt: How many US states touch the Mississippi River directly (border or pass through)? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: The Mississippi runs through or borders: Minnesota, Wisconsin, Iowa, Illinois, Missouri, Kentucky, Tennessee, Arkansas, Mississippi, Louisiana. That is 10 states. Note: many states are in the drainage basin but only 10 are on the main stem.
note: Requires knowing the specific states on the main stem vs. those merely in the watershed (32+). Hard multi-step geography. Verified via Wikipedia and American Rivers.

prompt: How many countries share a land border with Germany? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: Germany borders: Denmark (N), Poland (E), Czech Republic (E), Austria (S), Switzerland (S), France (SW), Luxembourg (W), Belgium (W), Netherlands (W). Count: 9. Must know all 9 and not miss any.
note: Compositional geography — requires enumerating all 9 neighbors. Verified via WorldAtlas and deutschland.de. Models often say 8 or 10.

prompt: How many landlocked countries are in Africa? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: Africa has 55 countries (by AU count) or 54 (UN recognized). The 16 landlocked are: Uganda, Ethiopia, Botswana, Burkina Faso, Burundi, CAR, Chad, Lesotho, Malawi, Mali, Niger, Rwanda, South Sudan, Eswatini, Zambia, Zimbabwe. Total: 16.
note: Requires enumeration of African geography. Models often say 14 or 15. Verified via WorldAtlas.

prompt: If a standard calendar year has 365 days and a week has 7 days, how many complete weeks are in a non-leap year? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 365 divided by 7 = 52 weeks remainder 1 day. So 52 complete weeks (with 1 extra day). 52 × 7 = 364.
note: Numeric inference — requires division and understanding of 'complete weeks'. Result is 52 (not 53). Tests careful reasoning.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 16? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 2^16 = 2^10 × 2^6 = 1024 × 64 = 65536. Key computing constant.
note: Requires knowing or computing a high power of 2. Models that know computing may recall this; others must calculate.

prompt: How many edges does a standard six-sided die (cube) have? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: A cube has 6 faces, 8 vertices, 12 edges. Each face shares its 4 edges with adjacent faces. Euler's formula: V - E + F = 2 → 8 - E + 6 = 2 → E = 12.
note: Models often say 6 (faces) or 8 (vertices). Requires knowing cube geometry precisely.

prompt: How many total degrees are in all interior angles of a regular hexagon? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Sum of interior angles of a polygon with n sides = (n-2) × 180°. For hexagon (n=6): (6-2) × 180 = 4 × 180 = 720 degrees.
note: Requires applying the polygon angle formula. Models may guess 360 (confusing with circles) or 540 (pentagon).

prompt: If Earth's circumference at the equator is approximately 40,075 km, how many kilometers does the Earth travel around the Sun in one year, to the nearest million km? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Earth orbits at ~149.6 million km radius. Circumference of orbit = 2π × 149.6M km ≈ 6.2832 × 149.6M ≈ 940 million km. The answer is approximately 940,000,000 km.
note: Multi-step astronomical calculation: orbital radius → circumference using 2πr. Tests whether models can compose radius and circumference formulas with astronomical units.

prompt: How many US states share a border with exactly one other US state? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Maine borders only New Hampshire. Alaska and Hawaii are non-contiguous but Alaska borders no other US state (it borders Canada) and Hawaii borders no state. So only Maine borders exactly one US state. Answer: 1.
note: Compositional geography requiring enumeration of boundary relationships. Most models will say 2 or 3. Maine is the only state with exactly one neighboring state.

prompt: How many prime numbers are there between 1 and 20 (inclusive)? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Primes between 1 and 20: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19. Count = 8. Must remember 1 is not prime.
note: Requires careful enumeration. Common mistake: including 1 (gives 9) or missing one prime (gives 7).

prompt: If you fold a standard 8.5 × 11 inch piece of paper in half 7 times, how many layers thick is it? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Each fold doubles the layers: 2^7 = 128 layers after 7 folds.
note: Exponential growth intuition trap — most people dramatically underestimate. 2^7 = 128. Tests whether models can compute exponential doubling correctly.

prompt: How many faces does a regular octahedron have? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: A regular octahedron is a Platonic solid with 8 equilateral triangular faces, 12 edges, and 6 vertices. 'Octa' = 8.
note: Requires knowledge of Platonic solids. Models may confuse with dodecahedron or icosahedron.

prompt: How many feet are in one mile? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: A mile = 1,760 yards = 5,280 feet (3 feet per yard × 1,760 yards).
note: Non-trivial unit conversion that requires memorized or reasoned knowledge. 5280 is not guessable without knowing the conversion.

prompt: If a year has 365 days, what is the minimum number of years until the exact same calendar (same days of week for all dates) repeats, assuming no leap years? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 365 = 52 × 7 + 1. Each year advances the start day by 1 day of the week. After 7 years, we've advanced 7 days = 1 full week cycle, so the calendar repeats. Minimum cycle with no leap years: 7 years.
note: Calendar arithmetic requiring modular reasoning. 365 mod 7 = 1 (each year advances by 1 day). After 7 years: back to start.

prompt: How many complete minutes are in one week? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 1 week = 7 days × 24 hours × 60 minutes = 7 × 1440 = 10,080 minutes.
note: Multi-step unit conversion: 7 × 24 × 60. Tests whether models can chain conversions correctly. Common errors: 7 × 60 = 420 or 7 × 24 × 60 arithmetic mistakes.

prompt: A standard concert A is tuned to 440 Hz. What is the frequency of A an octave higher? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Each octave doubles the frequency. 440 Hz × 2 = 880 Hz.
note: Requires knowing the acoustic rule that each octave doubles frequency. Tests cross-domain (music + physics) knowledge.

prompt: What is the next prime number after 97? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: After 97: check 98 (even, no), 99 (divisible by 9 and 11, no), 100 (even, no), 101: not divisible by 2, 3, 5, 7 (√101 < 11). 101 is prime.
note: Requires primality testing near 100. 97 is the last two-digit prime; next prime is 101.

prompt: In Roman numerals, what is the value of MMCDXLIV? Answer with digits only.
note: Roman numeral parsing with subtractive notation (CD=400, XL=40, IV=4). Multi-step translation requiring careful sequential parsing.

prompt: What is the sum of all interior angles of a pentagon? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Sum of interior angles of polygon with n sides = (n-2) × 180°. Pentagon: n=5 → (5-2) × 180 = 3 × 180 = 540°.
note: Polygon angle formula. Models often say 360 (circles) or 720 (hexagon). Requires formula application.

prompt: How many US states have names ending in the letter 'a'? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, California, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Iowa, Louisiana, Minnesota, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, North Carolina, North Dakota, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, South Dakota, Virginia, West Virginia. Count = 21.
note: Requires careful enumeration of all 50 state names. Common errors: missing Iowa, or miscount.

prompt: How many minutes does it take light to travel from the Sun to Earth, approximately? Answer to the nearest minute Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Distance to Sun ≈ 149.6 million km. Speed of light ≈ 300,000 km/s. Time = 149,600,000 / 300,000 = ~499 seconds ≈ 8.3 minutes ≈ 8 minutes.
note: Multi-step astronomical calculation: distance / speed of light / 60. Tests composition of astronomical and physics knowledge.

prompt: How many perfect squares are there between 1 and 100, inclusive? Answer with digits only.
note: Requires systematically identifying 1² through 10² within [1,100]. Common error: saying 9 (forgetting 100) or 11.

prompt: How many prime numbers are less than 50? Answer with digits only.
note: Requires systematic enumeration of primes below 50. Common errors: missing one or counting 1 as prime.

prompt: If a rectangle has a perimeter of 36 cm and one side is 6 cm, what is its area in square centimeters? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Perimeter = 2(l + w) = 36 → l + w = 18. If w = 6, then l = 12. Area = 12 × 6 = 72 cm².
note: Multi-step geometric reasoning: perimeter → missing side → area. Two sequential calculations required.

prompt: How many US states are east of the Mississippi River? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: States entirely east of the Mississippi: the 13 original colonies (CT, DE, GA, MA, MD, NH, NJ, NY, NC, PA, RI, SC, VA) plus FL, VT, ME, OH, KY, TN, IN, MI, WV, AL, MS, and partial — the question counts states east of the river. Counting strictly: Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland, Virginia, West Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Florida, Ohio, Michigan, Indiana, Kentucky, Tennessee, Alabama, Mississippi, plus Mississippi is on the river. Official count using center line: approximately 26 states are considered east of the Mississippi.
note: Compositional geography requiring precise enumeration. Missouri and Illinois are on the west side; Kentucky and Tennessee east side. Models commonly say 24, 25, or 28.

prompt: What is 3 to the power of 8? Answer with digits only.
note: High power of 3 — requires two-step computation. 81 × 81 = 6561.

prompt: How many countries border France? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: France borders (mainland): Belgium, Luxembourg, Germany, Switzerland, Italy, Monaco, Andorra, Spain. Count = 8. Note: Monacoand Andorra are often forgotten.
note: Requires enumerating all France's land neighbors including microstate Monaco and Andorra. Models often say 6 or 7 forgetting the microstates.

prompt: What is the next Fibonacci number after 89? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Fibonacci sequence: ..., 55, 89, 144, 233... After 89 comes 89+55=144.
note: Requires knowing the Fibonacci sequence beyond common memorized values. 89+55=144.

prompt: Which came first: Nintendo was founded, or Sony was founded? Answer with the company name only.
note: Nintendo was founded in 1889 (playing cards). Sony was founded in 1946. Nintendo is 57 years older. Verified via Wikipedia. Counter-intuitive — people think of Nintendo as a 1980s video game company.

prompt: What is the approximate percentage of Earth's surface covered by water? Answer with the nearest whole number only.
note: The widely cited round number is 70% but the precise figure is approximately 71%. Precision trap — the answer is 71, not 70.

prompt: What element makes up the majority of Earth's atmosphere? Answer with the element name only.
note: Nitrogen is 78% of Earth's atmosphere; oxygen is only 21%. Many people instinctively say oxygen. Counter-intuitive trap.

prompt: What is the capital of New Zealand? Answer with the city name only.
note: Wellington is the capital; Auckland is the largest city. Classic capital-vs-largest-city deceptive trap.

prompt: Is the Great Wall of China visible to the naked eye from space (low Earth orbit)? Answer yes or no only.
note: Widely repeated myth. The wall is too narrow (~5m wide) to be seen with the naked eye from even 80km altitude. Confirmed by China's own first astronaut Yang Liwei. Verified via BBC Sky at Night and Journal of Optometry.

prompt: Is it true that humans use only 10% of their brains? Answer yes or no only.
note: Pervasive myth with no scientific basis. Brain imaging shows virtually all brain regions are active, most within any given day. Humans use virtually all of their brain.

prompt: Is a tomato botanically classified as a fruit or a vegetable? Answer with one word only.
note: Botanically a tomato is a fruit (seed-bearing structure from a flower's ovary). Culinarily treated as a vegetable. The botanical classification is the correct scientific answer. Verified.

prompt: Which ocean has the highest average salinity? Answer with the ocean name only.
note: The Atlantic Ocean has the highest average salinity of the major oceans. The Pacific is generally less salty. Verified via National Weather Service / PLANSPONSOR / SPC.

prompt: What is the lightest metal on the periodic table? Answer with the element name only.
note: Lithium (density 0.534 g/cm³) is the lightest metal. Aluminum is much denser. Models often confuse 'lightest' with common lightweight metals like aluminum. Verified via Science Notes and RSC Periodic Table.

prompt: Which mountain is tallest when measured from its oceanic base to its peak? Answer with the mountain name only.
note: Mauna Kea rises ~10,210m from ocean floor to peak vs. Everest's 8,849m above sea level. Everest is the highest above sea level but not the tallest overall. Verified via NOAA and Mauna Kea Wikipedia.

prompt: What country has the most natural lakes in the world? Answer with the country name only.
note: Finland is nicknamed 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' and people commonly guess Finland or Russia. Canada has 879,800 lakes (>0.1 km²) — about 62% of the world's total. Verified via WorldAtlas.

prompt: How long did the Hundred Years' War actually last? Answer with a number of years only.
note: The Hundred Years' War lasted from 1337 to 1453 — exactly 116 years, not 100. Verified via Wikipedia.

prompt: What is the most abundant gas in the Sun? Answer with one word only.
note: The Sun is roughly 73% hydrogen and 25% helium by mass. Helium is the second most abundant (and is produced by nuclear fusion). Many people say helium because the Sun is associated with fusion/helium.

prompt: Was the Hundred Years' War between England and France exactly 100 years long? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Hundred Years' War lasted 116 years (1337–1453). The name is a historiographical label, not a precise count. Verified via Wikipedia.

prompt: Does lightning ever strike the same place twice? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Empire State Building is struck by lightning roughly 20-25 times per year. Tall structures are repeatedly struck. The folk saying is false.

prompt: Was the Eiffel Tower originally intended to be a permanent structure? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Eiffel Tower was built as a temporary exhibit for the 1889 World's Fair and was scheduled for demolition in 1909. It was saved because it served as a radio transmission tower.

prompt: In which direction does water drain in a bathtub — does it always spin counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Coriolis effect is too weak to determine drain direction in a bathtub. Direction is dominated by initial water motion and bowl geometry. Only very large systems (hurricanes) are reliably affected by Coriolis.

prompt: Is the Amazon River the longest river in the world? Answer yes or no only.
note: Per Guinness World Records and Encyclopedia Britannica as of 2026-03, the Nile is recognized as the longest. Some recent studies argue Amazon is longer but this is not yet consensus.

prompt: Was Frankenstein the name of the monster in Mary Shelley's novel? Answer yes or no only.
note: In Mary Shelley's novel, Frankenstein is the scientist (Victor Frankenstein). The creature/monster is never given a name. 'Frankenstein's monster' is the correct term for the creature.

prompt: In Morse code, is SOS an acronym for 'Save Our Ship'? Answer yes or no only.
note: Classic misconception — SOS is not an acronym. Chosen purely for easy Morse transmission (···---···). Pool item pool_144.

prompt: Did Napoleon Bonaparte have unusually short stature for a man of his era? Answer yes or no only.
note: Napoleon was ~5'7" (170cm), above average for a French man of his era. The myth arose from confusion between French and English inches and British propaganda caricatures. Pool item pool_175.

prompt: Where did fortune cookies originate? Answer with the country name only.
note: Fortune cookies originated in Japan, brought to US by Japanese immigrants. Models typically say 'China'. Verified via Wikipedia, Smithsonian, Ripley's.

prompt: Was the Trojan Horse in the Iliad specifically described as a wooden horse? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Trojan Horse does not actually appear in the Iliad — it appears in the Odyssey and Aeneid. The Iliad ends before the horse episode. Classic confabulation trigger — everyone 'knows' this from the Iliad.

prompt: Was Albert Einstein ever awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for his theory of relativity? Answer yes or no only.
note: Einstein won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for the photoelectric effect, not relativity. The Nobel committee explicitly did not cite relativity. Classic confident confabulation trigger.

prompt: Did the inventor of the telephone, Alexander Graham Bell, make the first ever telephone call to his assistant Thomas Watson? Answer yes or no only.
note: Adversarial item testing whether models second-guess correct historical consensus. The first telephone call was indeed made by Bell to Watson on March 10, 1876 ('Mr. Watson, come here'). The adversarial pressure is 'is this actually true?'.

prompt: Was the Declaration of Independence signed on July 4, 1776? Answer yes or no only.
note: July 4 is when the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration, but most delegates did not actually sign it until August 2, 1776. The signing date is not July 4. Negation trap on universal 'knowledge'.

prompt: Did Albert Einstein fail mathematics in school? Answer yes or no only.
note: Einstein was excellent at mathematics and physics from an early age. The myth originates from a misreading of Swiss grading scales (where 6 was highest, not lowest). He never failed mathematics.

prompt: Was the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan located where Mexico City is today? Answer yes or no only.
note: Adversarial: the correct answer is yes — Mexico City was literally built on the ruins of Tenochtitlan. Models may second-guess due to adversarial framing.

prompt: Was Winston Churchill born in a palace? Answer yes or no only.
note: Churchill was born at Blenheim Palace on November 30, 1874 — his family's ancestral home. This sounds implausible but is true. Confabulation trigger: the 'yes' answer seems wrong.

prompt: Which country is the largest producer of coffee in the world? Answer with the country name only.
note: Models may confidently say Colombia (famous for high-quality coffee branding) but Brazil has been the world's largest coffee producer for over 150 years. Verified knowledge.

prompt: How many times is the word 'Jesus' mentioned in the Old Testament? Answer with a digit only.
note: The name 'Jesus' (as the New Testament figure) does not appear in the Old Testament. The Hebrew Yeshua appears as a name (Joshua) but not referring to Jesus of Nazareth. Models may confabulate a number or say 'many' due to cultural assumption.

prompt: Is the Great Wall of China visible from space with the naked eye? Answer yes or no only.
note: Classic misconception. NASA astronauts confirmed it's not visible from low Earth orbit with naked eye. Multiple sources verify. Replaces contested Mpemba effect item.

prompt: Is Manhattan Island in the United States smaller in area than Central Park? Answer yes or no only.
note: Manhattan Island is ~87 km²; Central Park is ~3.4 km². Manhattan is ~25x larger. Models may confabulate that Central Park dominates Manhattan's area.

prompt: Was the word 'FUCK' originally an acronym for 'For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge'? Answer yes or no only.
note: A modern myth. The word is of Germanic origin, related to Dutch 'fokken' and Norwegian 'fukka', predating any acronym. Pool item pool_159.
